county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Corydon. Created 1808 from Clark and Knox counties. Corydon was Indiana's first state capital (1816-1825) — territorial and early state government records at Indiana State Archives. Harrison County Historical Society. Battle of Corydon (1863, Morgan's Raid) — Civil War records. Squire Boone Caverns area (Daniel Boone's brother). Ohio River county — ferry and steamboat records. Indian Creek and Blue River valleys. Constitution Elm site. Early settlers from Kentucky and Virginia. Strong Baptist and Presbyterian records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1808. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1808. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1808. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1808. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Harrison County Auditor, Corydon / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1808. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.source_name: Clark's Grant Land Records — Harrison County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives, Indianapolis / Indiana State Library
source_profiles.coverage_notes: George Rogers Clark's Illinois Regiment land grant (150,000 acres) in southeastern Indiana. Soldiers who served under Clark in the Revolutionary War received land warrants. This area was NOT part of the federal land system — use Virginia/Illinois records, not BLM-GLO. Early Clarksville (1784) was Indiana's first American settlement.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Records at Indiana State Archives and Indiana State Library; some published in Indiana land record compilations

source_profiles.source_name: Ohio River Commerce Records — Harrison County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Local historical societies / Kentucky river communities
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Steamboat records, ferry licenses, river pilot records, and flatboat commerce. Ohio River counties had close economic ties to Kentucky — cross-reference Kentucky county records across the river. Early Indiana settlement came primarily via the Ohio River.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Ferry licenses at county level; steamboat records at NARA and historical societies

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana provided approximately 197,000 soldiers to the Union Army. Adjutant General records, enlistment papers, and discharge records at Indiana State Archives. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis). W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols.) published rosters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Indiana Adjutant General records well-preserved at Indiana State Archives; indexed online

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Danville. Created 1823 from unorganized territory (Delaware). Hendricks County Historical Museum in Danville. Originally rural but now a fast-growing Indianapolis suburb. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Strong Quaker settlement — Plainfield Monthly Meeting records are extensive (Quaker collection at Plainfield Public Library). Danville and Brownsburg area records. White Lick Creek settlements. County Clerk holds marriage records from 1824. Check Indiana State Library for microfilmed records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1823. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1823. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1823. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil and criminal case files, order books, and judgments from 1823. Indiana Circuit Court is the general trial court. Early records include debt cases, property disputes, bastardy cases, and naturalization petitions (pre-1906) that identify family relationships. Order books often record road petitions naming landowners.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Court records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Hendricks County Auditor, Danville / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Tax duplicates list property owners, land descriptions, and property values from 1823. Indiana county auditor records. Early tax lists serve as census substitutes. Indiana State Archives holds some microfilmed county tax records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some tax duplicates at Indiana State Archives; others remain at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Hendricks County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.source_name: Society of Friends (Quaker) Meeting Records — Hendricks County
source_profiles.repository: Earlham College Friends Collection, Richmond / Indiana Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Monthly meeting records include births, marriages, deaths, removals (transfers), and disownments. Indiana Yearly Meeting and Western Yearly Meeting records. Quakers kept meticulous records. Indiana was a major destination for Quaker migration from the Carolinas and Virginia — check origin meeting records.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Exceptionally well-preserved; many microfilmed by FamilySearch and held at Earlham College

source_profiles.source_name: Underground Railroad and African American Records — Hendricks County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Archives / Indiana Historical Society / Levi Coffin House
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana was a free state but had Black Codes restricting African American settlement. Court records include freedom suits, fugitive slave cases, and Black Code enforcement. Levi Coffin of Fountain City (Wayne County) was the 'President of the Underground Railroad.' Quaker meeting records document abolitionist activity.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across local historical societies and Indiana State Archives; some published accounts

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in New Castle. Created 1821 from unorganized territory (Delaware). Henry County Historical Society in New Castle. Strong Quaker influence — Spiceland Academy and Friends meeting records. Indiana Soldiers' and Sailors' Children's Home (Knightstown) records. Indiana Basketball Hall of Fame in New Castle. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Blue River and Fall Creek valley settlements. Middletown, Knightstown, and Spiceland area records. Well-preserved county records from 1822.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1821. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1821. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Created 1844 from Cass, Carroll, Miami, Grant, Tipton.
county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Kokomo. Created 1844 from several parent counties. Kokomo was a major center of the Indiana natural gas boom (1880s-1890s) and later the automobile industry — Haynes-Apperson, Continental Steel, Chrysler/Stellantis records. Howard County Historical Society. Elwood Haynes pioneer automobile heritage. Wildcat Creek valley. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Strong industrial employment records from early 1900s. Check parent counties for pre-1844 records. Greentown and Russiaville area records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1844. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage applications, licenses, and returns from 1844. Indiana County Clerk issues marriage licenses. Applications include ages, birthplaces, and parents' names (improving detail over time).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Well-preserved at County Clerk office

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, guardianships, and estate inventories from 1844. Indiana probate is handled by the Circuit Court (County Clerk). Inventories often list personal property in detail and name heirs.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Probate records generally well-preserved at county level

source_profiles.repository: Bureau of Land Management, General Land Office Records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Howard County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. Patents record first purchaser from the government. Searchable online at glorecords.blm.gov. Cross-reference with county deed records for subsequent transfers.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.source_name: Automobile Industry Employment Records — Howard County
source_profiles.repository: Indiana State Library / Indiana Historical Society / Local historical societies
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana was a major early automobile manufacturing center. Companies include Studebaker (South Bend), Haynes-Apperson (Kokomo), Duesenberg (Indianapolis), and later GM, Chrysler plants. Employment records, company newsletters, and union records document worker families across central Indiana.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Scattered across local repositories; Indiana State Library and Indiana Historical Society hold some collections

source_profiles.repository: Howard County Clerk, Kokomo / NARA Chicago
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Declarations of intention, petitions for naturalization, and certificates. Pre-1906 records at county Circuit Court (County Clerk). Post-1906 copies at NARA Chicago. Indiana had significant German, Irish, Swiss, and later Eastern European and Mexican immigration.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Many Indiana naturalization records indexed on FamilySearch and Ancestry; some at Indiana State Archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indiana provided approximately 197,000 soldiers to the Union Army. Adjutant General records, enlistment papers, and discharge records at Indiana State Archives. Indiana Soldiers and Sailors Monument (Indianapolis). W.H.H. Terrell's 'Report of the Adjutant General of the State of Indiana' (8 vols.) published rosters.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Indiana Adjutant General records well-preserved at Indiana State Archives; indexed online

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Huntington. Created 1832 from Allen and Grant counties. Wabash River and Little River valley settlements. Huntington County Historical Society. Huntington University (formerly Huntington College, 1897) archives. Forks of the Wabash Historic Park. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Erie Canal connection — early canal-era records. Strong German and Scots-Irish heritage. Our Sunday Visitor Catholic publishing company in Huntington. Check Allen and Grant for pre-1832 records. Roanoke and Andrews area records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1832. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival — continuous records from county formation

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Original federal land patents for Huntington County. Indiana is a federal land state — most land was sold through federal land offices. source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete federal records — well-preserved and digitized at BLM-GLO

source_profiles.repository: NARA, Washington DC / Indiana State Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Miami Nation records including treaties, removal records (1846), land allotments, annuity rolls, and agent correspondence. Some Miami families received individual land allotments and remained in Indiana. Chief Richardville (Peshewah) and Meshingomesia's band records. Treaty of St. Mary's (1818), Treaty of Paradise Spring (1826).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal records at NARA Record Group 75; some at Indiana State Archives

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wabash and Erie Canal (longest canal ever built in North America, 468 miles) and Whitewater Canal construction records, land purchases, worker rolls, and canal-related property records. Canal workers included many Irish immigrants. Canal land grants at Indiana State Archives.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Canal Commission records at Indiana State Archives; some published by Indiana Historical Bureau

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Brownstown. Created 1815 from Clark, Washington, and Jefferson counties. Jackson County Historical Society. Brownstown was the site of a significant Shawnee and Miami village. Muscatatuck River and East Fork White River valleys. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Seymour was a major railroad junction — railroad employment and Reno Gang (first train robbery in US, 1866) records. Strong Methodist and Baptist church records. Crothersville and Medora area records. Check parent counties for pre-1815 records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1815. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Rensselaer. Created 1835 from White County. Jasper County is flat prairie land in northwest Indiana — late settlement compared to southern counties. Jasper County Historical Society in Rensselaer. BLM-GLO federal land patents. St. Joseph's College (1889-2017) archives in Rensselaer. Iroquois River and Kankakee River marsh area. Grand Kankakee Marsh drainage records document land transformation. Remington and Wheatfield area records. Check White County for pre-1835 records.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1835. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Portland. Created 1835 from Randolph and Delaware counties. Jay County Historical Society in Portland. BLM-GLO federal land patents. Salamonie River valley settlements. Strong Swiss and German Mennonite heritage in Geneva and Berne area (shares with Adams County). Indiana natural gas boom county — Portland glass industry records. Dunkirk Glass Company records. Pennville and Redkey area records. Check Randolph and Delaware for pre-1835 records. Well-preserved county records from 1836.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1835. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Madison. Created 1810 from Clark and Dearborn counties. Madison was one of Indiana's most important early cities on the Ohio River — Madison Historic District is a National Historic Landmark. Jefferson County Historical Society. First railroad in Indiana (Madison and Indianapolis Railroad, 1836). James F.D. Lanier Mansion. Steamboat and river commerce records. Hanover College (1827) archives. Strong German and Irish immigrant records. Underground Railroad activity. Cross-reference with Kentucky counties across the river.

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1810. County Recorder holds grantor/grantee indexes. Indiana deeds include warranty deeds, quit-claim deeds, and mortgage records. Federal land state — earliest deeds may reference BLM-GLO original patents.

county_metadata.research_tips: County Recorder in Vernon. Created 1816 from Jackson and Jefferson counties — one of the original counties at statehood. Jennings County Historical Society. Vernon was briefly considered as a state capital. Muscatatuck River valley. BLM-GLO federal land patents. North Vernon became a railroad center — railroad employment records. Strong Methodist and Baptist church records from early settlement. Scipio and Hayden area records. Well-preserved early county records. Check Jackson and Jefferson for pre-1816 records.
